title: OAuth2/OIDC for API Authorization
description: Use OAuth2 flows with OIDC identity tokens; standardized claims; delegated auth support.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: OAuth2/OIDC implementation costs vary by approach. Min=$0 for self-hosted open-source solutions (Keycloak, ORY Hydra) on existing infrastructure or using managed identity providers with generous free tiers (Auth0: 7,000 MAU free, AWS Cognito: 50,000 MAU free, Azure AD B2C: 50,000 MAU free). Max=$2000 represents production scenarios: (1) Managed SaaS at scale - Auth0 Developer Pro ($240/mo) + 50K additional MAU (~$500/mo), Okta Workforce ($2-5 per user/mo for 100-500 users = $200-2500), or (2) Self-hosted with HA infrastructure - 3x t3.medium instances ($100/mo), RDS PostgreSQL multi-AZ ($150/mo), load balancer ($25/mo), monitoring/logging ($50/mo). Enterprise scenarios with 100K+ MAUs or advanced features (fraud detection, custom domains, premium support) can exceed $5000/mo but represent outlier cases.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity implementation. OAuth2/OIDC requires: (1) Understanding multiple grant flows (authorization code, client credentials, PKCE, refresh tokens) - 20-40 hours learning/design, (2) Integration across frontend and backend services - token validation, claims parsing, scope enforcement - 40-80 hours development, (3) Security configuration - CORS, redirect URIs, token rotation, secure storage - 20-40 hours, (4) Provider setup and configuration (managed service or self-hosted deployment with HA, backups, SSL) - 20-40 hours, (5) Testing flows including edge cases and security scenarios - 20-30 hours, (6) Documentation and runbooks - 10-20 hours. Total: 130-250 developer hours at $150/hour blended rate = $19,500-37,500 for complete greenfield implementation. However, with AI coding assistants (GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT) reducing boilerplate by 40-60%, mature libraries (passport.js, Spring Security, MSAL), and reference implementations, realistic adoption cost is $3500-5000 for teams with moderate auth experience, representing 20-35 focused development hours plus integration/testing time.
provenance.licenseCost: OAuth2 and OIDC are open standards (RFC 6749, RFC 6750, OpenID Connect Core 1.0) with no licensing fees. Implementation options are predominantly open-source: Keycloak (Apache 2.0), ORY Hydra (Apache 2.0), IdentityServer (Apache 2.0/.NET), Authelia (Apache 2.0), or cloud-native managed services with pay-per-use models (no upfront license). Commercial options like Auth0, Okta, Ping Identity use subscription models (counted in monthly costs, not one-time licenses). Enterprise support contracts for open-source solutions (Red Hat SSO for Keycloak: $50-100K/year) are optional operational costs, not mandatory licenses. Standard implementation requires no proprietary software licenses.

provides.reasoning: OAuth2/OIDC provides authentication through standardized tokens (access tokens, ID tokens, refresh tokens) that verify user identity and session state. This is the core mechanism for establishing authenticated sessions.
OAuth2 explicitly handles API authorization through scopes and access tokens, enabling fine-grained access control to protected resources. This is a fundamental purpose of the OAuth2 protocol.

OAuth2 enables delegated authorization where resource owners grant third-party applications limited access to their resources without sharing credentials. This is the defining feature that distinguishes OAuth2 from simple authentication.

OIDC provides single sign-on capability across multiple applications by allowing users to authenticate once with an identity provider and reuse that authentication session. The ID token and session management features enable seamless SSO experiences.
OAuth2/OIDC resolves authenticated user identity through the ID token sub claim and UserInfo endpoint, providing a verified user identifier to the application.
OAuth2/OIDC carries role and scope claims in tokens that downstream services use to enforce role-based access control, making RBAC a direct output of this pattern.

requires.reasoning: OAuth2/OIDC requires an authorization server (identity provider) to authenticate users, issue tokens, validate credentials, and manage the authorization flow. This is an essential architectural dependency.

Secure storage is critical for protecting access tokens, refresh tokens, and ID tokens from unauthorized access and theft. Improper token storage is a major security vulnerability in OAuth2 implementations.

supports_nfr.reason: This pattern specifically implements OAuth2 flows with OIDC identity tokens for delegated authorization.
OAuth2/OIDC is compatible with both audit logging enabled and disabled configurations for API authorization.
OAuth2/OIDC is compatible with GDPR requirements when proper consent and token expiration policies are configured.
OAuth2/OIDC supports HIPAA-compliant applications when combined with appropriate token encryption and audit trails.
OAuth2/OIDC works with CCPA requirements through proper token claim management and user consent flows.
OAuth2/OIDC is compatible with SOX compliance when audit trails of authorization decisions are maintained.

supports_constraints.reason: OAuth2/OIDC is a protocol-level standard that works across all cloud providers and on-premises environments.
OAuth2/OIDC libraries and implementations are available in all major programming languages for token validation.
OAuth2/OIDC applies to any platform requiring user authentication or API authorization including web, mobile, and IoT.
OAuth2/OIDC works with or without caching, though token caching improves performance by reducing IdP calls.
OAuth2/OIDC authorization is compatible with both synchronous and asynchronous messaging patterns via bearer tokens.
OAuth2/OIDC secures AI inference APIs and non-AI APIs equally through token-based authorization.
OAuth2/OIDC is commonly used in multi-tenant applications via tenant claims but also works for single-tenant systems.
OAuth2/OIDC supports batch job authorization through service account tokens and client credentials flow.
OAuth2/OIDC authorization is independent of distributed transaction coordination for application data.
OAuth2/OIDC secures real-time streaming endpoints through bearer tokens in WebSocket or SSE connections.
OAuth2/OIDC authorizes vector search API requests independent of the search technology or embedding model.
OAuth2/OIDC works with any database type including document stores for API authorization.
OAuth2/OIDC is compatible with key-value store backends for application data access authorization.
OAuth2/OIDC authorizes graph database queries through API tokens independent of the graph traversal logic.
OAuth2/OIDC secures time-series database APIs through standard token-based authorization mechanisms.
OAuth2/OIDC supports OLTP workloads by authorizing transactional API requests with low-latency token validation.
OAuth2/OIDC authorizes OLAP analytical queries through API tokens with appropriate scope and claim-based access control.

oauth_flow.description: OAuth2 flow type. authorization_code for user-facing apps with backend, client_credentials for service-to-service, device_code for input-constrained devices, implicit for legacy SPAs (not recommended).

token_storage.description: Where to store access tokens. secure_storage uses platform keychain/keystore, memory_only for high-security short sessions, encrypted_storage for persistence, httponly_cookie for web apps to prevent XSS.

pkce_enabled.description: Enable Proof Key for Code Exchange. Required for mobile/desktop apps and SPAs to prevent authorization code interception attacks. Recommended for all public clients.

scope_strategy.description: How to request OAuth scopes. minimal requests only necessary permissions, role_based maps to user roles, resource_specific requests granular resource access.

token_refresh.description: Token refresh strategy. automatic refreshes before expiry, manual requires explicit refresh calls, sliding_window extends session on activity.

id_token_validation.description: OIDC ID token validation level. strict validates all claims including nonce/at_hash, standard validates signature and standard claims, relaxed for testing only.

requires_nfr.reason: OAuth2/OIDC can support up to four nines availability when combined with token caching and IdP redundancy.
OAuth2/OIDC adds at least 50ms p95 latency due to JWT signature verification and token validation overhead.
P99 latency includes occasional IdP network calls for token refresh or validation resulting in 100ms+ tail latency.
OAuth2/OIDC enables fast recovery of 1-5 minutes as auth state is externalized in tokens and IdP can restart quickly.
Pattern supports up to 10M jobs/hour when using local JWT validation with cached public keys for signature verification.

warn_nfr.reason: OAuth2/OIDC adds token validation overhead (JWT signature verification, claim parsing). At very low QPS (<1 req/s), simpler auth mechanisms like basic auth or API keys may be more appropriate.
warn_nfr.message: sec-auth-oauth2-oidc: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (<1). OAuth2/OIDC token validation overhead may be unnecessary; consider simpler auth like API keys for low-traffic APIs.
